common.selectItemToViewProperties: Select an item to view properties

elements.SCREEN_purpose: Visualizes the current state for the user.
elements.SCREEN_story: The "Scene". It displays information derived from past events (via Projections) and offers controls to trigger new actions (Commands).
elements.SCREEN_tech: Presentation Layer. The "Where" of the interaction.
elements.COMMAND_purpose: An intent to change the system state.
elements.COMMAND_story: The "Attempt". You are asking the system to do something. Before saying "Yes", the system builds a Decision Model to check if this is allowed.
elements.COMMAND_tech: Input / Request. Targeting specific Tags (Consistency Boundary) to validate Invariants.
elements.DOMAIN_EVENT_story: The "History". If this exists, it means the Command was accepted and all business rules (Decision Model) passed. It cannot be rejected or changed later.
elements.DOMAIN_EVENT_tech: Immutable Fact. The basis for all State (Projections).
elements.READ_MODEL_purpose: Translates event history into useful state.
elements.READ_MODEL_story: The "Translation". Events are just a list of what happened. This component translates that raw list into a specific shape—either for the UI (Read Model) or to check rules (Decision Model).
elements.INTEGRATION_EVENT_story: A signal, typically a message with an id, that something happened in another boundary (like a Payment Provider) that we need to react to.
elements.AUTOMATION_story: The "Robot". It watches for specific Events (facts) and automatically triggers a new Command (intent) to keep a process moving.

help.title: Help
help.loadExampleDescription: Click below to load a sample model and see how it works.
help.loadExampleError: Failed to load example project. Please try again.

introContent.title: Introduction
introContent.description: Event Modeling is a visual way to design systems by focusing on how information changes over time. You build a complete 'story' of your system with each chapter being a slice or building block told from left to right.

storage.title: Storage & Privacy
storage.localFirst: Local-First: Data is stored exclusively in your browser (IndexedDB). Weavr has no access to it.
storage.persistence: Persistence: Your data remains here as long as you don't clear your browser's 'Site Data' or 'Cache'.
storage.backups: Backups: To keep your data safe from accidental deletion, use the Export button in the top header to save a .json file to your computer.

stateChange.title: State Change Pattern
stateChange.description: State Change Pattern describes a state change and its way from the start (what is the trigger?) to the end (what is the state change?). It starts with a white box (Screen), followed by a blue box (Command) and then one or multiple yellow boxes (Event).

stateView.title: State View Pattern
stateView.description: State View Pattern connects existing events from the board to a green “Read Model (View)” box. That leads to a quick overview of what information will be used by it.

automation.title: Automation Pattern
automation.description: Use this pattern whenever the system should do something automatically. It is essentially a combined State Change and State View Pattern with an automated trigger in the middle.

translation.title: Translation Pattern (System integration)
translation.description: Used for transferring knowledge between systems. An external Integration Event triggers an Automation that issues a Command to produce an outgoing Integration Event. External data can also populate Read Models directly for visualization.

leftChair.title: The Left Chair
leftChair.description: One command triggering too many events. This often happens when business logic is crammed into one place instead of being broken down into separate state changes.

rightChair.title: The Right Chair
rightChair.description: Many events feeding into a single read model (View). This indicates a 'Summary View' that knows everything, potentially creating high coupling.

bed.title: The Bed
bed.description: One UI component firing multiple commands in sequence. This reveals orchestration happening in the front-end instead of letting the event flow handle the sequence.

bookshelf.title: The Bookshelf
bookshelf.description: One slice contains all your business rules and logic (Given-When-Thens), while others are anemic. This is a 'God-Object' in visual form where one slice does everything.

semanticsContent.title: Modeling the Data Flow
semanticsContent.description: Weavr is more than just a modeling tool — it has a built-in logic engine for system specification where you can model input, output, and data dependencies between elements. Here is the workflow for modeling the data flow:

dictionary.title: 1. Data Dictionary: Define your Language
dictionary.description: Use the sidebar's Data Dictionary to define all the core concepts (entities and fields) your system needs to know. This is your glossary and your 'Source of Truth'.

fields.title: 2. Properties: Use your Concepts in the Event Model
fields.description: When you double-click an element on the canvas, or use the shortcut <kbd>ALT+E</kbd>, you open the Properties panel where, under the 'Data' section, you can specify the data fields the element uses. You can choose to create new ones or reuse fields you've created in your Data Dictionary.
fields.required: Required: You can mark properties as required or mandatory for the business logic.
fields.roles: Screen: You can choose whether a Screen element is used for data 'Input' or 'View'.

completeness.title: 3. Information Completeness Check: Does your story add up?
completeness.description: Weavr automatically monitors your design. Every data field in your elements must be traceable to its origin (data-lineage), i.e., where it was first registered in the system, either via a Screen input or an Integration Event.
completeness.example: Screen 'Order' is missing data from Read Model: OrderID
completeness.note: This ensures that you have a coherent data flow, where you always have control over the system's dependencies, before you even begin writing the code.

aggregates.title: 4. Aggregates
aggregates.description: Aggregates are collections of elements that exist within the same context and are used to validate changes in the system's state. Domain Events are grouped into lanes based on their associated aggregate.

slices.title: 5. Slices
slices.description: Slices represent the smallest functional building blocks in the system, and each slice is based on one of the 4 patterns in Event Modeling. They group related functions into a timeline, creating coherence in the system. Use Slices to divide a large model into manageable, logical, and loosely coupled components that can be developed and tested independently.

controlsContent.pan: Pan: Scroll anywhere, or hold <kbd>Space</kbd> + Drag (Hand tool).
controlsContent.deselectAll: Deselect All: Click once on the empty canvas background.
controlsContent.addElement: Add Element: Click the + button or press <kbd>A</kbd> / <kbd>N</kbd>.
controlsContent.quickAdd: Quick Add: With toolbar open, press <kbd>1</kbd>-<kbd>6</kbd> to add specific elements.
controlsContent.selectAll: Select All: Press <kbd>Ctrl/Cmd</kbd> + <kbd>A</kbd> to select all elements.
controlsContent.singleSelect: Single Select: Click any element to select it.
controlsContent.moveSingle: Move Single Element: Click and drag a single element.
controlsContent.moveMultiple: Move Multiple Elements: Select multiple elements, then drag them with the cursor or use the <kbd>Arrow Keys</kbd>.
controlsContent.delete: Delete: Select one or more elements and press <kbd>Delete</kbd> or <kbd>Backspace</kbd>.
controlsContent.navigate: Navigate: Use <kbd>Tab</kbd> and <kbd>Shift</kbd>+<kbd>Tab</kbd> to cycle through elements.
controlsContent.focusNode: Focus Node: Press <kbd>F</kbd> to center the view on the selected element.
controlsContent.duplicate: Duplicate: Select elements and press <kbd>D</kbd> or <kbd>Ctrl/Cmd</kbd> + <kbd>D</kbd>.
controlsContent.copyPaste: Copy/Paste: Press <kbd>Ctrl/Cmd</kbd> + <kbd>C</kbd> to Copy, <kbd>Ctrl/Cmd</kbd> + <kbd>V</kbd> to Paste.
controlsContent.openPanel: Open Panel: Double-click an element, or select one and press <kbd>Enter</kbd>.
controlsContent.closePanel: Close Panel: Press <kbd>Esc</kbd> or click the 'X' button.
controlsContent.multiEdit: Multi-Edit: Select multiple elements (<kbd>Shift</kbd>+Drag or <kbd>Shift</kbd>+Click) to assign Slices or Entities to all of them at once.
controlsContent.jumpToRelationships: Jump to Relationships: Select a node and press <kbd>C</kbd>, or click the + handle.
controlsContent.drawConnection: Draw Connection: Drag the + handle to another node.
controlsContent.quickDraw: Quick Draw: Hold <kbd>Alt</kbd> and drag from a node.
